So if the story had started and finished with her being off the boat her life would exactly equal as it was after she was in the boat, because it was not her family and she had no stakes in it at all. They could have taken a psychological horror angle to the story it would have been more interesting.

Matter fact, i saw people gave bad review of the book well i cannot deny certain moment in the book kinda boring and lacking for example theorizing things that remain unanswered and unnecessary. However the movie managed to take a turn for the worse than the book itself. Also another frustration to add; the director and all cast members didn’t read the book at all! But shoutout to director’s wife who had read the book and saw the ending from the book could’ve been better and they gave justice to it.

video_title: The Woman in Cabin 10 | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Act I – Origins in Shadows
	•	Opens in Japan, a cinematic retelling of Daken’s origin: born of Wolverine and Itsu, raised under deception. His childhood shows manipulation, his claws first emerging in a scene that parallels but contrasts Logan’s own.
	•	His adoptive mentor (Romulus or a new mystic figure) teaches him not just brutality but a twisted sense of destiny.
	•	The mysticism comes through Japanese folklore—dreams, ancestral visions, whispers of the Wolverine’s bloodline carrying a curse/blessing.

⸻

Act II – The Hunt for the Father
	•	As a young adult, Daken travels the world chasing fragments of Wolverine’s story. He’s conflicted: does he want revenge, or understanding?
	•	His path takes him into mutant enclaves, hidden temples, and eventually toward the Xavier Institute, which becomes a symbolic crossroads:
	•	Does he embrace his father’s world of heroes?
	•	Or fall to the shadows his father fought against?
	•	Here, Daken’s powers—claws, healing, AND his pheromone manipulation—make him dangerous but also alluring.

⸻

Act III – Becoming More than the Shadow
	•	A mystic confrontation—perhaps with Romulus or another force tied to Wolverine’s bloodline—forces Daken to choose.
	•	Instead of simply being Wolverine’s son, he carves his own path—neither villain nor copycat.
	•	Final act: Daken takes a stand as a new kind of hero, one who embraces the darkness inside him but fights to shape it into something greater.

⸻

Casting Ideas 🎭

Daken needs an actor who can pull off intensity, vulnerability, and raw charisma. Possible picks:
	•	Lewis Tan (Mortal Kombat, Into the Badlands) – martial arts background, brooding charisma.
	•	Andrew Koji (Warrior) – has the quiet intensity, physicality, and emotional depth.
	•	Aramis Knight (Into the Badlands) – younger option, can play conflicted and dangerous.
	•	Mena Massoud (Aladdin) – brings charm and intensity, unexpected but intriguing.

⸻

Why it Works
	•	Audiences want a next generation Wolverine story—but not just a repeat of Logan. Daken brings that continuation while being fresh, mystic, and morally complex.
	•	It opens up the Japanese mystic side of the Marvel universe, adding depth and style.
	•	It can connect to the X-Men revival in the MCU while also standing alone as a character-driven drama.
